neighborhoods.name: Bronzeville / Black Belt
primary_groups: African Americans from Mississippi, Alabama, Arkansas, Tennessee
neighborhoods.character: Dense residential corridor along South State Street between 26th and 47th Streets. Restrictive covenants and racial violence confined Black residents to a narrow strip that became one of the most densely populated urban areas in the country. Despite the crowding, Bronzeville developed a thriving cultural and commercial life.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Chicago around {year}, Bronzeville was already a city within a city — the Defender had been telling Southern readers to come north since 1916, and by {year} the South Side was home to more transplanted Mississippians than some Mississippi counties.

neighborhoods.name: Back of the Yards / Packingtown
primary_groups: Irish, German, Lithuanian, Polish, Czech, Mexican immigrants
neighborhoods.character: Working-class neighborhood immediately west of the Union Stock Yards. The smell of the stockyards permeated everything. Houses were frame cottages and two-flats, often without indoor plumbing until the 1920s. The neighborhood turned over ethnically every generation as each group moved up and out.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} worked the yards, {pronoun_subject} likely lived in Back of the Yards, where the reek of the stockyards settled over the rooftops like fog and the parish church anchored every block.

neighborhoods.name: Polish Triangle / Polish Downtown
primary_groups: Polish immigrants from Prussian, Russian, and Austrian partitions
neighborhoods.character: Centered around the intersection of Milwaukee, Ashland, and Division streets in the Wicker Park and Bucktown area. By 1900, Chicago had more Polish residents than any city except Warsaw. The neighborhood was anchored by massive Catholic churches that served as community centers, banks, and schools.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The {surname} family probably settled near one of the great Polish churches — St. Stanislaus Kostka alone had over 40,000 parishioners by 1900, making it the largest Catholic parish in the world.

neighborhoods.name: Maxwell Street / Near West Side
primary_groups: Eastern European Jews from Russia, Poland, Lithuania, Romania
neighborhoods.character: Chicago's primary Jewish immigrant quarter. Pushcart vendors lined the streets, tenement density rivaled the Lower East Side. Hull House, Jane Addams' settlement house, operated nearby serving this community and neighboring Italian and Greek enclaves.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} arrived from Eastern Europe in the {decade}s, Maxwell Street was likely the first neighborhood — Yiddish was the language of commerce, and a new arrival could find a peddler's cart and start work the same day.

major_employers.name: Union Stock Yards / Meatpacking
major_employers.working_conditions: Cold, wet, dangerous — workers stood in blood and offal for ten-hour shifts. Upton Sinclair's The Jungle documented the conditions in 1906. Unionization through the Amalgamated Meat Cutters improved conditions after the 1930s.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The stockyards offered {ancestor_name} steady if brutal work — the killing floor did not care where you came from, only that you could stand the pace.

major_employers.name: Pullman Company / Railroad Car Manufacturing
major_employers.wages: Pullman porters earned $27.50/month (1920s) plus tips; factory workers earned comparable industrial wages
major_employers.working_conditions: Pullman built an entire company town on Chicago's South Side. Porters were exclusively Black men with grueling schedules but social prestige. The Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters, organized by A. Philip Randolph in 1925, became the first Black-led union recognized by a major corporation.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} worked as a Pullman porter, the job meant weeks away from home but a foothold in the Black middle class — and the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters was training ground for a generation of civil rights leaders.

major_employers.name: International Harvester / McCormick Works
major_employers.wages: Industrial wages averaging $2-4/day (1900s), rising with union contracts through the mid-20th century
major_employers.working_conditions: Heavy manufacturing with significant injury risk. Site of labor conflicts leading to the 1886 Haymarket affair. Workers organized through the United Farm Equipment Workers and later the UAW.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The McCormick Works employed thousands of immigrant workers — if {ancestor_name} had mechanical skills, International Harvester was likely among the first places {pronoun_subject} applied.

major_employers.name: U.S. Steel South Works
major_employers.wages: $3-5/day (1910s), rising to $1.50+/hr (1950s) — among the highest industrial wages
major_employers.working_conditions: Extreme heat near furnaces, dangerous heavy machinery, twelve-hour shifts until the 1923 reforms. Over 20,000 employed at peak during WWII.
major_employers.narrative_hook: South Works stood at the mouth of the Calumet River like an industrial fortress — if {ancestor_name} worked steel, the glow of the furnaces was visible for miles.

port_of_entry_details.facility: Chicago was an inland destination. European immigrants arrived via Castle Garden (pre-1892) or Ellis Island (1892-1954) in New York, then transferred by rail — typically the New York Central, Pennsylvania Railroad, or Erie Railroad.
port_of_entry_details.processing: Immigrants arriving in Chicago had already cleared port-of-entry processing. Chicago's rail terminals — Union Station, Dearborn Station, LaSalle Street Station — served as de facto arrival points. Labor agents and ethnic boarding house operators met trains to recruit and house new arrivals. Southern Black migrants arrived primarily via the Illinois Central Railroad from Mississippi, Alabama, and Louisiana.
records_generated: Passenger manifests at port of entry (New York, Boston, Philadelphia, Baltimore)
Illinois Central Railroad records for Great Migration arrivals

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Boarding houses near stockyards or factories, shared flats in tenements, or company housing in planned communities like Pullman. Overcrowding was severe — families of six in two rooms were common.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Ethnic enclaves formed around parish churches and mutual aid societies. The Catholic parish was the organizing unit for Polish, Irish, Lithuanian, Czech, and Italian communities. Jewish settlement followed synagogue affiliation. Black residents were confined by covenants to the South Side Black Belt.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Boarding house (6-12 months) to shared flat (2-5 years) to two-flat ownership (first generation) to bungalow in outlying neighborhood or suburb (second generation). Black families faced a truncated trajectory — restrictive covenants blocked movement until the 1950s.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Each immigrant wave faced hostility from predecessors. Irish faced Know-Nothing violence in the 1850s. Eastern Europeans faced nativist legislation. Black migrants faced the most sustained resistance, including housing bombings and the 1919 race riot.
specific_incidents: 1855 Lager Beer Riot (German/Irish vs. nativist mayor)
1919 Race Riot — 38 dead, 537 injured, 1,000+ Black families homeless
1951-53 Cicero and Trumbull Park housing riots
nativist_organizations: Kenwood and Hyde Park Property Owners' Association (anti-Black, 1920s-1940s)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Stanislaus Kostka Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death registers in Polish and Latin from 1867. Parish census records. Records held by Archdiocese of Chicago Archives.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The parish records at St. Stanislaus Kostka may contain baptism or marriage entries for the {surname} family — the church served over 40,000 parishioners at its peak and recorded nearly every Polish Catholic life event on the Northwest Side.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Olivet Baptist Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community, Great Migration arrivals
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism records. The church grew from 4,000 members in 1915 to over 12,000 by 1930 as Great Migration arrivals joined.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Olivet Baptist Church on South Parkway was where newly arrived Southern families often landed first — the church ran employment services, housing referrals, and social programs that probably touched the {surname} family if they attended.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Polish National Alliance
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership applications with birthplace, date of arrival, occupation, and family information. Insurance policy records.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was Polish, the Polish National Alliance membership rolls may document their arrival date, birthplace in Poland, and occupation — the PNA was both a fraternal organization and an insurance provider that recorded biographical details on every applicant.

source_references.author: James R. Grossman
source_references.title: Land of Hope: Chicago, Black Southerners, and the Great Migration
source_references.notes: Definitive academic study of the Great Migration to Chicago, documenting chain migration patterns, labor recruitment, and community formation in Bronzeville.

source_references.author: Dominic A. Pacyga
source_references.title: Chicago: A Biography
source_references.notes: Comprehensive urban history covering all immigrant communities, industrial development, and neighborhood formation from the city's founding through the 20th century.

source_references.author: Thomas J. Jablonsky
source_references.title: Pride in the Jungle: Community and Everyday Life in Back of the Yards Chicago
source_references.notes: Detailed study of the stockyards neighborhood, covering immigrant succession, working conditions, parish life, and labor organizing.
